sentences.text: Yes.
My name is Raymonde Fiol, born Nathansohn.
OK.
My name is-- it's spelled F-I-O-L.
My maiden name is spelled N-A-T-H-A-N-S-O-H-N.
In 1936 in Paris, France.
August 22, 1936.
My father's name was David Nathansohn.
My mother's name was Esther Nathansohn.
They were both from Poland, although my father was born in Russia.
I believe my grandmother was traveling and happened to be in Russia at the time.
My father was from Warsaw.
And my mother was, I believe, near Warsaw, but I cannot pronounce the name.
No, I don't.
No, not really.
I, I do not remember pre-war extended family.
I've had-- I met family after the war, but do not recall anyone before the war.
As far as I know, my mother's family were all in Poland.
None survived.
The whole village-- the whole town was exterminated.
And none had left Poland to Western Europe.
My father had-- his-- no, my grandmother was already dead.
But his brothers-- he had five of them.
One was in England.
He survived the war.
One was in Palestine at the time.
He also survived.
One was in Italy in hiding.
He had lived in France and went to Italy.
And he was protected by the Italians.
And the other two did not survive.
Yes.
My Uncle Leon in London-- he's the one that survived the war.
Yes.
Leon Nathansohn.
The one in Palestine's name was Jakob Nathansohn.
The one that was saved in Italy's name is Gabriel Nathansohn.
And the-- I know of one uncle that did not survive.
His name was Isaac Nathansohn.
And he was taken, I believe, when he came back to France.
He used to be a, a wandering Jew, travel all over the world, and made the bad move of coming back to Europe from the United States at the wrong time.
They-- I believe they met in Paris.
Probably to find a better world.
I'm not sure.
He was a, a pocketbook maker.
A dressmaker.
Do you want the address? 22 Rue de Turenne.
[LAUGHS] Rue, R-U-E.
And the next word is D-E.
And then Turenne-- T-U-R-E-N-N-E.
Yes.
It's called the Marais, which is an old part of Paris.
And it was an interesting area of Paris.
Old-- it's an old part, very old.
It's an-- it was an apartment.
I remember the floor-- third floor.
I remember the apartment distinctly.
As a matter of fact, I was thinking about it recently.
It was a fairly big apartment.
Then there was a big living room overlooking a yard.
I guess I must have been playing, looking out as I remember that.
And then another back bedroom, I guess, and the bathroom in the back of the house, facing the other side of the building.
And I remember the balcony and looking over a terrace over the first floor.
I remember because I threw a bracelet once.
And the bracelet was on the terrace.
I could see it, but obviously could never retrieve it.
Don't recall any.
I do not recall.
I believe my father was an opera buff and would-- I remember that.
He would just have his little stool and just get online before the opera opened and-- just little, little things come to mind.
No, no.
I believe my mother may have worked at home.
I remember a sewing machine.
So I believe she did work at home, yeah.
I must have, yes.
Yes, because I recall being-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my mother was late picking me up.
So I remember crying and the teacher staying with me-- not to worry, that my mother would come and get me, or something.
So I must have been in school.
There's a basically pre-kindergarten school in France.
And there still is.
So you-- as soon as a child is trained, you could go to school.
So I remember that.
I remember crossing the street one time.
My mother sent me for eggs.
And I asked-- she told me to ask someone to cross-- to cross me, which I did.
And I remember-- and I brought the wrong eggs.
I remember that.
They were brown eggs.
And she wanted white eggs.
Yeah, but other than that, very little memories of pre-war France.
No.
No.
No, it's very-- no, I don't.
I really don't-- Yes.
I remember fighting with my cousin.
Yes.
Well, actually it's not a cousin.
It's my-- it's my aunt's-- aunt-by-marriage sister's daughter, who is very close.
And she wanted my red boots and my fur coat.
So it must have been wintertime.
And she's a couple years older.
And she just wanted them off my back.
[LAUGHTER] And we still talk about it.
No, absolutely not.
Not really.
Just little tidbits, not really.
I remember just very, very little.
No, no.
I believe my parents at that point were more-- very, very liberal, having come out of-- my father's mother was a rabbi.
And I think it was the time where the young people just left Poland and totally avoided religion, I believe.
So there was no, no involvement in, in the religious background at that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as far as I can remember.
It was a--
I believe they belonged to a choir.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Like I said, my father may have had a nice voice.
I don't recall.
And I think they did-- they were-- they did belong to a choir.
But I don't recall either seeing them there or-- I just remember mention of it, or how they-- or when they went, for that matter.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No, I guess they were busy working and trying to make a living and settle in a foreign land.
And no, I really don't-- maybe walks in the street.
Pre-war?
Not really.
No, not really.
It's hard.
I guess during the war, certain things-- just little incidents.
Well, when we were walking in the streets with-- I was walking in the street with my mother.
And a German officer stopped us.
She was wearing the Star of David.
And I wasn't.
And the officer wanted to know why.
And she had to prove that I was very young, and I did not have to.
But she had to show him my birth certificate of some kind to, to prove that.
Yes, she did.
Yes, she did.
No, no, I, I assumed he was speaking German.
And having been brought up with speaking Yiddish, I understood.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Another time, I remember the sirens going off.
And we had to run to the basement.
And I had wear a mask, I guess a gas mask.
I guess that's left over from the World War I.
But people had to.
And one incident when my-- we were on the train with my parents-- I was on the train with my parents.
And we were ready to leave on the train.
I'm not sure the-- where to.
I was told that later.
It was Switzerland.
And my father said, no.
Let's go home.
They're going to catch me anyway.
We got off the train.
And subsequently, I guess, they were taken to a labor camp.
I do not recall leaving Paris at that time.
No.
No.
No.
I remember being in a house.
And I think we were in the Ardennes where it was forced labor.
My parents were transported, including me.
And we were given a house to live in.
Well, we went-- we never went to Switzerland.
What happened was ultimately, my parents were arrested and taken to a labor camp.
Including me, yes.
Yes.
I remember wandering around the village by myself, I guess while they were working, and just playing in the streets or whatever.
I was left alone, I guess, while they had to work.
I believe they worked the land.
I remember my father's hands being dirty and, you know, scraped and stuff.
So I believe they had to work the land.
No.
No, I believe-- I think we had, like, a separate entrance.
And it was an upstairs-- we were living upstairs.
And I don't-- I, I-- it couldn't have been very big.
I think I was sleeping, like, on a small cot in an alcove.
And I don't remember very much about it.
No.
I don't think they spoke very much about it in front of me.
Yes, yeah, yeah.
No, I don't recall.
My vivid memories are more [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] past when they left me.
Well, they dropped me off with a Gentile family.
Yes.
But again, we were on the train first to Switzerland, got off that train, never made it.
Because if we had gone to Switzerland, my-- we, as a family, were allowed into Switzerland.
I believe Switzerland had a law that allowed young-- very young children and their parents for safety.
And we would have been allowed.
But my father was reluctant to go, thinking he would get caught.
So later on, I don't know.
I do not recall being taken from Paris into the labor camp.
No, no.
I don't.
Yes.
Yes, yeah.
No.
It had to have been in, in the Ardennes because my parents were-- when they found a family to hide me with, I remember we walked.
We never took any kind of transportation.
So it had to have been in the Ardennes area.
But I don't remember where.
No.
No.
Yes.
Yes, yes, yes.
I remember, you know, seeing strange people and people I was not used to because they were villagers that maybe dressed differently.
[LAUGHS] I remember seeing this lady all dressed in black.
[LAUGHS] And I ran.
Because I had never seen-- she was, like, wearing a long dress.
And maybe it was the way she looked.
I don't know.
Anyway-- but I remember being left on my own.
So I don't-- I don't recall going to school at that time.
No, not in that village.
No, I don't.
I do remember always 'Achtung!' on the radio.
As they went on, they would cut in and blabber something.
So I remember the word, 'Achtung!
Achtung!' on the radio-- I guess because it was occupied France.
And they would just, I don't know, go on and give instructions to people.
I'm not sure what.
But that's about all I can recall probably.
Yes.
Yes.
But I think probably we had our limits.
But I wasn't going to-- I'm sure I had an instruction not to go, you know, no further than maybe certain areas.
And I must've followed them.
And then my parents were working.
No.
Could have been a couple of years.
But I don't-- I had no schooling, as far as I can remember, in that village.
No.
Yes.
I remember my father I guess just crying.
[SOBBING] I do.
Yeah.
I do.
Frightened.
[SOBBING] Very frightened.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had a feeling my mother was pregnant, too.
And I think she may have lost the baby or was pregnant at the time when they were deported.
I'm not sure.
I remember that vividly.
[CRYING]
I think-- I think maybe they must have said something.
And my father was very upset.
Maybe they didn't know what to do about it.
That's the only incident that I remember about that.
[CRYING] That's all.
The next step would be that they told me, we're going to take a walk and go someplace.
No.
Probably could have been spring.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was no snow on the ground because we walked without any problem.
And they brought me to this village and introduced me-- met with this family.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I spent some time there.
And then they told me they were going to leave me there.
[CRYING] They said they would come back and get me.
[SOBBING] But they never did.
[SOBBING] I just believed them.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the people that took me in were very nice, very loving.
They had a--
Mr. and Mrs.
Cailac.
I don't know their first names.
C-A-I-L-A-C.
And--
I called them-- they asked me to call them, godfather and godmother, which I did-- 'parrain' for godfather, and 'marraine' for godmother.
And they had a daughter.
Their daughter must have been, at the time, near her 20s.
Her name was Evelyn.
And they had a cafe.
They ran the cafe.
And so they ran-- they-- and the house was their house.
They lived right in-- behind the house.
And she used to disappear-- the daughter used to disappear at night.
She used to bring supplies-- because they had a cafe, they had access to food-- and she used to bring supplies to the French Free Forces.
They were hiding in the mountains.
And, and then she would come back during-- in the morning.
Yes, I was aware of that, yes.
She had told me, I believe she did, yeah, and told me to keep quiet.
And I did.
I never mentioned anything.
I recall they-- when I first came, they had to take me and register with the Germans.
And unfortunately, I was not prompted.
And they asked me to give my name.
So I gave my name.
And--
I said, Raymonde Nathansohn.
And I was not supposed to.
I was supposed to give their names.
And just by giving my name, I gave away that I was Jewish.
And I saw their faces.
I could have killed us all at the same-- at that very time.
And the commandant said something to the effect that I looked like his daughter and let it go, and, I guess, let me stay there, but under their watchful eye.
I must have been maybe five, five or six at that point because I remember starting school.
I used to walk 10 miles a day to go to school.
Excuse me.
The village-- was really a village-- was Raucourt.
R-A-U-C-O-U-R-T.
It was several miles from Sedan in eastern-- in the Ardennes in France.
And where we-- the house was located was not even-- it was just a crossroads.
There were only two houses.
And it was called La Bagnole, L-A B-A-G-N-O-L-E.
Yes.
I had to walk summer, winter.
And I remember walking in heavy snows like what we have outside right now.
And--
There was a, a, a boy in the next house.
And sometimes, he'd walk with me.
Or I'd walk on my own.
And I'd go into town and just go to school.
Of course, I was also made to go to catechism in order to cover, basically, my, you know, my identity.
And--
I believe they said I was a relative from the city.
And because there was no food in the city and because of the war situation, they were just taking me in.
So I believe the Germans looked the other way in that respect because I had given my name.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
No, not at all.
Absolutely not.
I was-- this is-- this is really my first real recollection of facing a German in uniform, more like on a one-on-one basis, yeah.
And they were around.
And they had-- because of the cafe, they would come and drink in their cafe.
And they-- I--
The Germans would come in to the people where I lived with.
And he insisted I sit on his lap.
The German commandant.
Like I said, I looked like his daughter.
And I didn't want to.
And I was told that I better do that.
Yes.
Yes, yeah.
And--
Yes.
I remember asking them when they would come back.
They said, they'll come back.
Wait till the war's over.
They'll come back.
But they never did.
And--
Well, not really.
But war was all around us.
I remember bombings.
I remember Mr.
Cailac being in a truck, and-- he had his own truck.
And he was driving.
And pamphlets had been thrown from the planes.
It was the Americans saying, if you put a flag on your-- on the top of your truck that the-- you will not be bombed.
We'll try and avoid bombing you.
But obviously, it didn't work because he was bombed.
We were not bombed on the building itself.
But while he was on the road, he was-- he, he barely survived.
But--
Yes, he did, yeah.
So I remember bombings around us and leaflets being dropped.
And at some point in time, we started seeing the Germans retreating.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I assume at that point, it was when it was getting near the end of the war.
And the-- it lasted for seven days and seven nights.
